back: Reports that summarize a business’s financial activity and position. The main ones are the income statement, balance sheet, statement of owners equity, and statement of cash flows.
A report that shows whether a business made money over a period of time. Net Income = Revenue − Expenses.
A report that shows what a business owns, owes, and has left for owners at a specific date. Assets = Liabilities + Owners Equity.
A report that explains how the owners’ claim in the business changed during a period. It usually includes investments, net income or loss, and withdrawals or dividends.
A report that shows how cash came in and went out during a period. It separates cash flows into operating, investing, and financing activities.
Resources a business owns or controls that are expected to provide future benefit. Examples include cash, inventory, equipment, and buildings.
Amounts a business owes to others. Examples include loans, accounts payable, wages payable, and taxes payable.
The owners’ claim on the business after debts are subtracted. Owners Equity = Assets − Liabilities.
The basic rule that keeps accounting records balanced. Assets = Liabilities + Owners Equity.
Money earned from selling goods or providing services. Revenue increases net income and usually increases equity.
Costs used to earn revenue during a period. Expenses decrease net income and usually decrease equity.
The profit a business earns when revenues are greater than expenses. Net Income = Revenue − Expenses.
The result when expenses are greater than revenues. Net Loss = Expenses − Revenue.
A business owned by one person. The owner and business are legally closely connected, so the owner is personally responsible for business debts.
A business owned by two or more people. The partners usually share profits, losses, and responsibility for the business.
A business legally separate from its owners. Owners usually have limited liability and own the company through shares of stock.
Protection that keeps owners from usually being personally responsible for business debts. In practice, owners can lose what they invested but not usually their personal assets.
A person or organization that owns shares of a corporation. Stockholders are owners of the company.
Another name for a stockholder. A shareholder owns shares of a corporation.
The main accounting rules used by companies in the United States. GAAP helps companies prepare financial statements in a consistent way.
Accounting rules used in many countries outside the United States. IFRS helps companies around the world prepare financial statements in a comparable way.
A method that records revenues when earned and expenses when incurred, not necessarily when cash changes hands. This gives a better picture of business performance during a period.
A method that records revenue when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid. It is simpler but often gives a less accurate picture of performance.
A 12-month accounting period used by a business. It does not have to match the calendar year.
The specific span of time covered by a financial report. Examples include a month, quarter, or year.
The assumption that a business will continue operating into the future. This matters because many assets are recorded as useful over time instead of being valued as if the business is shutting down.
The rule that many assets are recorded at what the business originally paid for them. This makes records easier to verify.
The assumption that accounting records only include items that can be measured in money. In the United States, financial statements are usually reported in dollars.
The rule that the business’s finances are kept separate from the owner’s personal finances. This helps users see what belongs to the business itself.
The rule that companies should provide important information users need to understand the financial statements. This often appears in notes attached to the statements.
A list of all accounts a business uses to record transactions. It works like a menu of categories for accounting records.
A record used to track increases and decreases in one specific item. Examples include Cash, Accounts Payable, Sales Revenue, and Rent Expense.
An entry on the left side of an account. Debits increase assets and expenses, but decrease liabilities, equity, and revenue.
An entry on the right side of an account. Credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenue, but decrease assets and expenses.
The side of an account where increases are usually recorded. Assets and expenses normally have debit balances, while liabilities, equity, and revenue normally have credit balances.
A recording system where every transaction affects at least two accounts. Total debits must always equal total credits.
The first formal record of a transaction in accounting. It lists the accounts affected, the amounts, and whether each account is debited or credited.
The place where journal entries are first recorded in date order. It shows the story of transactions before they are moved to account records.
The full collection of all accounts used by a business. It shows the running balance for each account.
A simple drawing of an account shaped like a T. Debits go on the left and credits go on the right.
The process of moving amounts from journal entries into the ledger accounts. Posting updates each account’s balance.
A list of accounts and their balances used to check whether total debits equal total credits. It helps catch recording errors before statements are prepared.
A trial balance prepared before adjusting entries are made. It shows account balances after regular transactions have been recorded and posted.
The process of recording a transaction as a journal entry. It identifies which accounts are debited and credited.
Evidence that supports a transaction. Examples include receipts, invoices, checks, bank statements, and sales slips.
A journal entry with more than two accounts. Even with several accounts, total debits still must equal total credits.
Adding a column of numbers to get a total. In accounting, footing is often used to total debits or credits.
Marking records so a journal entry can be traced to the related ledger account. It helps prove that transactions were posted correctly.
An account that reduces the balance of a related account. For example, Accumulated Depreciation reduces the recorded value of equipment.
An account whose balance carries forward from one period to the next. Assets, liabilities, and equity accounts are permanent accounts.
An account that is reset to zero at the end of the period. Revenue, expense, and dividend accounts are temporary accounts.
The repeated process accountants use to record transactions and prepare financial statements. It starts with transactions and ends with closing the books for the period.
The process of deciding how a business event affects accounts. It identifies what increased, what decreased, and which accounts need debits or credits.
Entries made at the end of a period to update accounts before financial statements are prepared. They help record revenues and expenses in the correct period.
A trial balance prepared after adjusting entries are posted. It provides the updated account balances used to prepare financial statements.
Entries made at the end of the period to reset temporary accounts to zero. They move the period’s profit or loss into retained earnings or owner’s equity.
A trial balance prepared after closing entries are posted. It contains only permanent accounts because temporary accounts have been reset.
A temporary account used during the closing process. It collects revenues and expenses before the net income or loss is moved to equity.
Payments of company profits to stockholders. Dividends reduce retained earnings but are not expenses.
The amount of a corporation’s past profits kept in the business instead of paid out as dividends. Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends.
Optional entries made at the beginning of a new period to undo certain adjusting entries. They make later routine entries easier to record.
A working paper used to organize trial balances, adjustments, and financial statement amounts. It helps accountants prepare statements but is not itself a formal financial statement.
A company that buys and sells goods to customers. Its income statement includes sales revenue, cost of goods sold, and gross profit.
A company that earns revenue by providing services instead of selling goods. Examples include consulting firms, repair shops, and tutoring businesses.
The cost of inventory that was sold to customers during the period. Gross Profit = Sales Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold.
The profit left after subtracting the cost of goods sold from sales revenue. Gross Profit = Sales Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold.
The regular costs of running a business, not including the cost of the goods sold. Examples include rent, salaries, utilities, and advertising.
An income statement that separates major sections such as gross profit, operating income, and net income. It gives more detail about how profit was earned.
An income statement that groups all revenues together and all expenses together. Net Income = Total Revenues − Total Expenses.
A cost paid in advance before the benefit is used. It starts as an asset and becomes an expense over time.
Cash received before goods or services are provided. It starts as a liability and becomes revenue when the company does the work.
An expense that has been incurred but not yet paid or recorded. It is recorded with an expense and a liability.
Revenue that has been earned but not yet received in cash or recorded. It is recorded with revenue and an asset.
The process of spreading the cost of a long-term asset over the years it is used. It recognizes that assets like equipment wear out or lose usefulness over time.
The total depreciation recorded on a long-term asset so far. It reduces the asset’s reported value on the balance sheet.
The asset’s cost minus total depreciation recorded so far. Book Value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation.
The estimated amount a company expects to receive for an asset at the end of its useful life. It is used when calculating depreciation.
The estimated length of time a company expects to use a long-term asset. Useful life helps determine how much depreciation is recorded each period.
A method that records the same amount of depreciation each year. Annual Depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life.
The idea that expenses should be recorded in the same period as the revenues they helped earn. This helps show the real profit for a period.
The idea that revenue should be recorded when it is earned, not necessarily when cash is received. In practice, this means recording revenue when the company has provided the goods or services.
A situation where cash is paid or received before the related expense or revenue is recorded. Examples include prepaid expenses and unearned revenue.
A situation where revenue or expense is recorded before cash is received or paid. Examples include accrued revenue and accrued expenses.
Money owed to employees for work they have already done. It is a liability until the company pays the employees.
Interest cost a company owes but has not yet paid. It is recorded as a liability.
Interest a company has earned but has not yet collected. It is recorded as an asset.
The cost of supplies used up during the accounting period. It is recorded so the remaining supplies asset is not overstated.
The cost of insurance coverage used during the period. It is often recorded by reducing prepaid insurance.
The cost of using rented space or property during a period. It is recorded when the rent applies to that period.
The portion of a long-term asset’s cost assigned to the current period. It reduces net income but does not directly use cash in that period.
The estimated cost of customers not paying what they owe. It helps match credit sales with the expected loss from unpaid accounts.
A balance sheet that groups assets and liabilities into current and long-term sections. This makes it easier to judge liquidity and solvency.
Assets expected to be used, sold, or turned into cash within one year or one operating cycle. Examples include cash, accounts receivable, inventory, and supplies.
Assets expected to benefit the business for more than one year. Examples include land, buildings, equipment, and long-term investments.
Debts expected to be paid within one year or one operating cycle. Examples include accounts payable, wages payable, and short-term notes payable.
Debts that are not due within the next year. Examples include long-term loans, bonds payable, and long-term lease obligations.
The amount by which current assets exceed current liabilities. Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities.
A measure of whether a company has enough short-term assets to cover short-term debts. Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities.
A company’s ability to pay short-term debts as they come due. Cash and other current assets are important for liquidity.
A company’s ability to survive financially over the long run. Solvency focuses on whether the company can handle its long-term debts.
The owners’ claim on a corporation after liabilities are subtracted from assets. Stockholders Equity = Assets − Liabilities.
The basic ownership shares issued by a corporation. Common stockholders usually have voting rights and may receive dividends.
The amount stockholders paid for stock above its stated or par value. It is part of stockholders’ equity.
The corporation’s accumulated profits kept in the business instead of paid out as dividends. Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends.
A broader measure of changes in equity from non-owner sources. It includes net income plus certain gains or losses that do not appear directly on the income statement.
A measure of profit earned for each share of common stock. Earnings Per Share = Net Income Available to Common Stockholders ÷ Average Common Shares Outstanding.
The amount of dividends paid for each share of stock. Dividends Per Share = Total Dividends ÷ Shares Outstanding.
Extra explanations that provide important details behind the financial statement numbers. They can explain accounting choices, risks, debt terms, and other useful information.
Financial statements that show more than one period side by side. They help users see how the company changed over time.
A method that shows each financial statement item as a percentage of a base amount. On an income statement, each item is often shown as a percentage of sales.
A method that compares financial statement amounts across periods. Percentage Change = (New Amount − Old Amount) ÷ Old Amount.
Financial statements that show items as percentages instead of only dollar amounts. They make it easier to compare companies of different sizes.
Policies and procedures designed to protect assets and improve reliable reporting. In practice, they help prevent mistakes, theft, and fraud.
Dividing important tasks among different people. This makes it harder for one person to both commit and hide an error or fraud.
Approval given before a transaction or action takes place. For example, a manager may need to approve large purchases.
Ways to physically protect assets and records. Examples include locks, safes, passwords, cameras, and restricted access.
Checking work by someone who did not originally do it. This helps catch errors and discourage dishonest behavior.
The process of comparing the company’s cash records with the bank statement. It explains differences and updates the cash account.
Checks written by the company that have not yet cleared the bank. They are subtracted from the bank balance during a bank reconciliation.
Deposits recorded by the company that have not yet appeared on the bank statement. They are added to the bank balance during a bank reconciliation.
Fees charged by the bank for account services. They reduce the company’s cash balance and must be recorded in the company’s books.
A customer’s check that the bank refuses because the customer does not have enough money. NSF stands for not sufficient funds.
A small amount of cash kept on hand for minor purchases. It is used for things like postage, small supplies, or quick reimbursements.
The specific amount of money set aside for small cash payments. The fund is replenished when receipts are reviewed and cash is added back.
The person responsible for managing and protecting a petty cash fund or other asset. The custodian keeps records and provides receipts for payments.
Short-term, highly liquid investments that can quickly be turned into cash. They are usually very safe and mature in three months or less.
Cash that is set aside for a specific purpose and not freely available for general use. Companies often disclose restrictions so users understand the cash balance.
A U.S. law passed to improve corporate reporting and reduce financial fraud. In practice, it requires stronger internal controls and more responsibility from top executives.
A model explaining why fraud is more likely when pressure, opportunity, and rationalization are present. Internal controls mainly try to reduce opportunity.
Stealing money or property that a person was trusted to manage. For example, an employee taking company cash for personal use is embezzlement.
Buying insurance protection against losses caused by employee theft or dishonesty. It also often requires background checks before employees handle cash.
